title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
Sparsity As A Prior
Week 9 introduced ISTA:

x^{k+1}
=
S_{\tau\lambda}
\left(x^k-\tau A^\top(Ax^k-y)\right).

The soft-thresholding step imposes a modeling assumption: many coefficients should be zero or nearly zero.
crumbs: Part IV: Sparse and Learned Priors

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
Why This Chapter Matters
The previous chapters introduced regularization as a way to stabilize inverse problems. Tikhonov prefers small energy. Total Variation prefers sparse gradients. Sparse reconstruction generalizes this idea: a signal or image may be simple after the right representation is chosen.
This is an important turn in the course. We stop asking only whether an image is smooth and begin asking where its information is concentrated. If most of the meaningful structure can be described by a small number of coefficients, then incomplete measurements may be enough to reconstruct it.
This also prepares the path to neural networks. A neural network can be understood, partly, as learning a representation in which relevant image structure becomes easier to describe, separate, or reconstruct. Sparse reconstruction is a hand-designed predecessor of that idea.
A vector x\in\mathbb{R}^n is k-sparse if it has at most k nonzero entries:

\|x\|_0
=
\#\{i:x_i\neq 0\}.

The notation \|\cdot\|_0 counts nonzeros; it is not a true norm.


Sparse vectors have only a few active coefficients; dense vectors spread information across many entries.

Sparsity is stronger than smallness. A vector can have small entries everywhere and still be dense. Sparse modeling says that most entries are exactly zero or negligible, and a relatively small set carries the important information.
In practice, exact sparsity is rare. Approximate sparsity is enough for many imaging methods: sorted coefficient magnitudes decay rapidly, so keeping the largest coefficients captures most visible structure.
crumbs: Part IV: Sparse and Learned Priors

title: Sparse Reconstruction
crumbs: Part IV: Sparse and Learned Priors

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
Analysis And Synthesis Views
There are two common ways to use a transform.
In the synthesis view, the image is built from coefficients:

x=\Psi c.

The reconstruction problem may become

\min_c
D(y,A\Psi c)+\lambda\|c\|_1.

In the analysis view, we penalize transformed coefficients of the image:

\min_x
D(y,Ax)+\lambda\|\Psi x\|_1.

For orthogonal transforms these views can be closely related. For redundant transforms, learned dictionaries, or gradient penalties, they can behave differently. The important point is that sparsity is not a property of pixels alone; it is a property of pixels plus a representation.
crumbs: Part IV: Sparse and Learned Priors

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
l2 Regularization
Tikhonov-style regularization uses

\frac12\|Ax-y\|_2^2
+
\frac{\lambda}{2}\|x\|_2^2.

The l2 penalty prefers small energy spread across coefficients. It is smooth and mathematically convenient, but it does not specifically ask for exact zeros.
When many vectors fit the data, l2 often chooses a dense small-norm solution.
crumbs: Part IV: Sparse and Learned Priors

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
l1 Regularization
Sparse reconstruction often uses

\frac12\|Ax-y\|_2^2+\lambda\|x\|_1,

where

\|x\|_1=\sum_i |x_i|.

The l1 penalty encourages exact zeros. It is convex but nonsmooth.
The ideal sparse model might be

\min_x \|x\|_0
\quad\text{subject to}\quad
Ax=y.

This problem is combinatorial. The l1 norm is a convex relaxation that preserves much of the sparse behavior.
The word “relaxation” means that we replace a hard, discrete problem by a continuous one that is easier to optimize. The l0 problem asks which subset of coefficients should be active. The l1 problem avoids explicit subset search, while still encouraging many coefficients to become zero.
This is a remarkable compromise: l1 is convex, so it can be optimized reliably in many settings, but it still behaves like a sparsity prior.
crumbs: Part IV: Sparse and Learned Priors

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
Geometry Of l1 And l2


The corners of the l1 ball make sparse solutions more likely.

The l2 ball is round. The l1 ball has corners on coordinate axes. When a data constraint first touches an l1 ball, it often touches a corner, which means some coordinates are exactly zero.
This geometric picture matches the algorithmic picture from soft thresholding.
When students first see this figure, the key is to focus on the contact point. The data constraint represents all coefficient vectors that fit the measurement equally well. The regularizer chooses the smallest ball that touches that constraint. A round l2 ball has no preference for axes. The l1 diamond has corners on axes, so its contact points often remove coefficients.
The geometry is a picture of the prior.
crumbs: Part IV: Sparse and Learned Priors

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
Underdetermined Recovery
Suppose we observe

y=Ax,

where A has fewer rows than columns. If m<n, there are usually many possible solutions.
One classical choice is the minimum l2-norm solution:

\min_x \|x\|_2
\quad\text{subject to}\quad
Ax=y.

This fits the data, but it may be dense.
The l1-regularized solution

\min_x
\frac12\|Ax-y\|_2^2+\lambda\|x\|_1

balances data fit with sparse coefficients.


The same measurements can lead to very different reconstructions under different priors.

Both methods see the same A and y. The difference is the prior: l2 asks for a small vector, while l1 asks for a sparse explanation.
This is the recurring inverse-problem lesson. Data alone may be compatible with many answers. The regularizer chooses among them. Sparse reconstruction chooses the answer that can be explained using few active components.
This can be extremely powerful, but it can also be wrong. If the true image is not sparse in the chosen representation, the sparse answer may look artificial or may erase structure that matters.
crumbs: Part IV: Sparse and Learned Priors

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
Compressed Sensing Intuition
If x\in\mathbb{R}^{1000} is exactly 5-sparse, it is not truly a 1000-degree-of-freedom object. We need to learn which 5 locations are active and the 5 active values.
Sparse recovery can work with far fewer than n measurements, but not usually as few as k. We need enough measurements to identify support and amplitudes robustly.


l1 recovery can improve sharply once enough measurements are available.

Sparse recovery can fail even when the true signal is sparse. The representation must be appropriate, the measurement operator must mix information well, and the regularization weight must be chosen carefully.
The measurement operator matters because some measurements are more informative about sparse coefficients than others. Random measurements often behave well in theory because they mix information broadly. Structured measurements can be excellent or terrible depending on how they interact with the sparse basis.
For example, if the measurements miss exactly the locations where the sparse signal is active, recovery is impossible. If the measurement system confuses two sparse patterns, no regularizer can reliably distinguish them without additional information.
Compressed sensing is therefore not “recover anything from almost nothing.” It is a set of conditions under which sparse objects can be recovered from fewer measurements than classical intuition would suggest.
crumbs: Part IV: Sparse and Learned Priors

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
Sparse Images
For images, sparsity is usually transform-domain sparsity.
The Discrete Cosine Transform represents an image patch using cosine patterns. Many patches can be approximated using only a small fraction of DCT coefficients.


Few large transform coefficients can preserve much of the visual structure.

Suppose we observe only a random subset of pixels. We can reconstruct using sparse DCT coefficients by solving

\min_c
\frac12\|M D^{-1}c-y\|_2^2+\lambda\|c\|_1.

Here D^{-1}c maps DCT coefficients back to an image, and M keeps the observed pixels.
This formula is worth reading slowly:

c is the unknown sparse coefficient vector;
D^{-1}c turns coefficients into an image;
M simulates the pixels that were actually observed;
M D^{-1}c-y is the data mismatch;
\|c\|_1 asks for a sparse transform-domain explanation.



Sparse priors can fill in missing data when the transform model is appropriate.

This is not a universal promise. The representation, measurements, noise level, calibration, model mismatch, and regularization weight all matter.
When reading the reconstruction figure, compare three things: observed pixels, filled-in smooth regions, and fine details. Sparse DCT priors often reconstruct broad structure well, but they can struggle with localized sharp details or texture. This is not surprising: DCT atoms are global cosine patterns, not localized edge atoms.
crumbs: Part IV: Sparse and Learned Priors

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
l1 Versus l2



Feature
l2
l1




Geometry
round
corners


Typical coefficients
many small values
many zeros


Optimization
smooth
nonsmooth


Prior
small energy
sparse explanation


Useful when
dense small errors
few active structures
crumbs: Part IV: Sparse and Learned Priors

title: Sparse Reconstruction
section: From Sparse Priors To Learned Representations
text:
From Sparse Priors To Learned Representations
Sparse reconstruction asks us to choose a representation before reconstruction. DCT and wavelets are fixed mathematical transforms. Dictionaries can be learned from data. Neural networks go further: they can learn multiple layers of features adapted to a reconstruction task.
The conceptual line is:

\text{pixels}
\longrightarrow
\text{fixed transform coefficients}
\longrightarrow
\text{learned dictionary}
\longrightarrow
\text{deep learned representation}.

In each case, we hope that the representation separates meaningful structure from noise, ambiguity, or missing data. What changes is how flexible the representation is and how easy it is to interpret.
Fixed sparse priors are transparent but limited. Learned representations are expressive but can be harder to analyze. This tradeoff will become central in the later chapters.
crumbs: Part IV: Sparse and Learned Priors

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
Common Mistakes
A first mistake is to say that sparsity is an intrinsic property of an image. Sparsity depends on the representation.
A second mistake is to believe that l1 always recovers the true sparse signal. Recovery depends on the measurement operator, noise level, sparsity level, and parameter choice.
A third mistake is to equate compression quality with reconstruction reliability. A representation that compresses natural images well may still fail for a particular inverse problem if the measurements do not contain the right information.

Takeaways

Sparsity means few nonzero or significant coefficients.
Images are sparse only after choosing a representation.
l1 is a convex surrogate for sparse selection.
l1 and l2 encode different priors.
Sparse reconstruction works when the measurement model and sparse representation are compatible.
Learned imaging methods can be seen as replacing fixed sparse transforms with trainable representations.

title: Total Variation Regularization
crumbs: Part III: Variational Models and Optimization

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
Edges Are Not Noise
Quadratic smoothing uses

E(u)
=
\frac12\|u-y\|_2^2
+
\frac{\lambda}{2}\|\nabla u\|_2^2.

This removes noise, but it also penalizes true edges. Edges and noise both create local changes, so a model must distinguish between unwanted oscillation and meaningful discontinuity.
Total Variation changes the regularizer.
crumbs: Part III: Variational Models and Optimization

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
Why This Chapter Matters
Chapter 7 showed that quadratic smoothness is mathematically clean but visually limited. It treats large gradients as very expensive, so it tends to blur object boundaries. Total Variation is the first regularizer in the course that is explicitly designed around a basic fact of images: important information often lives at edges.
This is also the first strong example of a nonsmooth prior. The mathematical difficulty is not an accident. The corner in the absolute value is exactly what makes TV able to suppress many small gradients while allowing some large ones.
TV therefore teaches two lessons at once:

good image priors should match the structures we want to preserve;
better priors often require better optimization tools.
crumbs: Part III: Variational Models and Optimization

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
Continuous Total Variation
For a grayscale image u:\Omega\to\mathbb{R},

\operatorname{TV}(u)
=
\int_\Omega |\nabla u(x)|\,dx.

TV measures the total amount of change in the image.
For a pixel grid, the isotropic discrete TV is

\operatorname{TV}(u)
=
\sum_i
\sqrt{(D_xu_i)^2+(D_yu_i)^2}.

Here D_x and D_y are finite differences in horizontal and vertical directions.
There are two common discrete variants. Isotropic TV uses the Euclidean magnitude

\sqrt{(D_xu_i)^2+(D_yu_i)^2}.

Anisotropic TV instead uses

|D_xu_i|+|D_yu_i|.

Both encourage small total variation, but they can behave differently with respect to diagonal edges and grid directions. In this course, isotropic TV is the default because it is less tied to horizontal and vertical axes.
crumbs: Part III: Variational Models and Optimization

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
TV Denoising
The classical Rudin-Osher-Fatemi model is

u_\lambda
=
\operatorname*{argmin}_u
\frac12\|u-y\|_2^2
+
\lambda\,\operatorname{TV}(u).

The data fidelity term keeps the reconstruction close to the noisy data. The TV term controls the total amount of change.
This regularizer says: among images that fit the data, prefer images with small total variation.
For Gaussian denoising, the ROF model uses a squared-error fidelity because the noise assumption is Gaussian. If the data were photon counts, we would usually replace the fidelity term with a Poisson likelihood while keeping a TV prior:

\operatorname*{argmin}_u
D_{\text{Poisson}}(y,u)
+
\lambda\operatorname{TV}(u).

This is the modular power of variational modeling. The data model and image prior can be changed separately.
crumbs: Part III: Variational Models and Optimization

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
l2 Versus TV Penalties
For a one-dimensional gradient value g, compare:

\text{quadratic penalty}=\frac12 g^2,
\qquad
\text{TV penalty}=|g|.



Large gradients are much more expensive under a quadratic penalty than under TV.

For example, if g=4, then \frac12g^2=8, while |g|=4. The difference becomes larger as |g| grows.
This is the basic reason TV preserves edges better: it penalizes the existence of change, but it is less aggressive toward large jumps than a squared-gradient model.
The comparison should be read as a statement about priorities. A quadratic gradient penalty says that one large jump is much worse than several small changes. TV is more tolerant of a concentrated jump. For images containing objects, this is often desirable: an edge should remain an edge, not be spread into a blurry ramp.
At the same time, TV does not know which edges are meaningful. It may preserve strong noise spikes or remove weak true details depending on the parameter and the data. Edge preservation is a modeling tendency, not a guarantee of semantic correctness.
crumbs: Part III: Variational Models and Optimization

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
Nonsmoothness
The function |g| is not differentiable at g=0. This is not a minor technicality. It changes the optimization problem.
Quadratic smoothing leads to smooth linear equations. TV denoising is convex but nonsmooth. That makes it powerful for edge preservation and more delicate to solve.
Optimization methods for nonsmooth convex problems appear in the next chapter.
One common classroom workaround is to replace |\nabla u| by a smoothed approximation:

\sqrt{|\nabla u|^2+\epsilon^2}.

This makes derivatives easier to write, but it also slightly changes the model. As \epsilon becomes smaller, the approximation is closer to TV and the optimization becomes more delicate. This is a recurring theme: numerical convenience and modeling fidelity must be balanced.
crumbs: Part III: Variational Models and Optimization

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
One-Dimensional Edge Preservation
TV is easiest to understand on a step signal.


Quadratic smoothing rounds jumps, while TV keeps flatter regions and sharper transitions.

TV is well matched to piecewise-constant structure. It counts the amount of change, not how spread out the change is. A sharp jump can be cheaper than a long gradual transition with many small changes.
This is useful when the image contains objects separated by edges.
The same property can be expressed as sparsity of the gradient. TV does not usually make the image itself sparse. Instead, it encourages the gradient image to be sparse: many pixels have nearly zero gradient, and relatively few pixels carry transitions.
This idea prepares the next part of the course. Sparse reconstruction generalizes the question: in which representation should an image be simple? TV answers: the gradient representation. Wavelets answer differently. Neural representations answer differently again.
crumbs: Part III: Variational Models and Optimization

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
Image Denoising
The contrast between quadratic smoothing and TV also appears on images.


TV removes noise while preserving stronger edge sets better than quadratic smoothing.

Gradient maps make the behavior easier to see.


Noise creates many small gradients. TV suppresses weak fluctuations while retaining stronger edges.

When reading the gradient maps, compare density rather than only brightness. The noisy image has gradients almost everywhere. The TV result has a more selective gradient map: many weak fluctuations disappear, while some coherent boundaries remain.
This selectivity is why TV can improve segmentation, object boundaries, and cartoon-like reconstructions. It is also why TV can damage textures, because texture often consists of many meaningful gradients.
crumbs: Part III: Variational Models and Optimization

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
Choosing The TV Weight
In

\frac12\|u-y\|_2^2+\lambda\operatorname{TV}(u),

larger \lambda means:

stronger denoising;
fewer small variations;
greater risk of losing detail.



The best value of \lambda depends on the task.

For a photograph, a visually pleasing balance may be appropriate. For a diagnostic image, oversmoothing a small structure may be unacceptable. Parameter choice is therefore both mathematical and domain dependent.
The parameter \lambda has the same conceptual role as in Tikhonov, but the visual effect is different. Increasing \lambda does not simply blur the image. It tends to remove weak oscillations and simplify the edge set. This can look like denoising, segmentation, or cartoonization depending on the image and the parameter.
crumbs: Part III: Variational Models and Optimization

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
Staircasing
TV favors sparse gradients. That means many locations with zero gradient and a smaller number of jumps.


TV can turn smooth variation into piecewise-flat plateaus.

This staircasing effect is the signature artifact of TV. TV preserves edges, but it can create artificial plateaus in regions that should vary smoothly.
Staircasing is not a bug in a particular implementation. It follows from the prior. TV rewards sparse gradients, so a smooth ramp can be approximated by flat regions separated by jumps. If the true image contains gentle shading, smooth surfaces, or illumination gradients, pure TV can be too aggressive.
Higher-order regularizers and combined models were developed partly to reduce this artifact.
crumbs: Part III: Variational Models and Optimization

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
Strengths And Weaknesses








Property
l2 smoothing
TV




Noise removal
yes
yes


Edge preservation
weak
strong


Optimization
smooth linear problem
nonsmooth convex problem


Typical artifact
blur
staircasing


Best for
smooth images
piecewise-smooth or piecewise-constant images



TV may struggle with texture, fine repeated patterns, gradual shading, curved smooth surfaces, and strongly nonlocal structure.
This is not a failure of mathematics. It is a reminder that every regularizer is a modeling choice.
crumbs: Part III: Variational Models and Optimization

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
TV And Learned Priors
TV is a hand-designed prior. It expresses a clear belief: good images often have sparse gradients. Neural priors are usually less explicit. They may learn from many examples that natural images contain edges, textures, repeated patterns, object shapes, and correlations across distant regions.
This comparison is useful:








Prior
What it can express
Main risk




TV
sparse gradients and sharp boundaries
staircasing, loss of texture


Wavelet sparsity
multiscale structure
basis mismatch


Learned prior
complex training-set statistics
hallucination or dataset bias



TV is therefore not made obsolete by neural networks. It is a simple, interpretable example of a prior. It helps us ask the right question about learned priors: what structure are they imposing, and when should we trust it?
crumbs: Part III: Variational Models and Optimization

crumbs: Part III: Variational Models and Optimization

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
Common Mistakes
A first mistake is to say that TV preserves all edges. TV preserves some strong transitions better than quadratic smoothing, but weak edges can still disappear and noise edges can remain.
A second mistake is to think that TV is always more accurate because it looks sharper. Sharpness is not the same as truth.
A third mistake is to ignore staircasing. If the true image contains smooth shading, TV may replace it with artificial plateaus.

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
Takeaways

Total Variation uses \operatorname{TV}(u)=\int_\Omega |\nabla u|\,dx.
TV favors images with small total change.
Compared with l2 smoothing, TV preserves stronger edges.
TV is convex but nonsmooth.
TV can create staircasing artifacts, so it is not always the right prior.
TV is an interpretable prior, useful both practically and as a reference point for learned priors.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text: Slides | Notebook | Open in Colab

title: Tikhonov Regularization
Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Why Regularize?
Chapter 5 showed that direct inversion can amplify noise through terms such as

\frac{\langle \eta,u_i\rangle}{\sigma_i}.

When \sigma_i is small, division by \sigma_i is dangerous. Regularization changes the inverse problem so that weakly observed directions are not trusted blindly.
For a linear imaging model

y=Ax+\eta,

the Gaussian least-squares data fit is

\|Ax-y\|_2^2.

If A is ill-conditioned, least squares can fit noise. Data fit alone does not know which data components are reliable.
Tikhonov regularization is the first full example of the course template

\operatorname*{argmin}_x D(Ax,y)+\lambda R(x).

Here the data term comes from the Gaussian noise model of Chapter 4, and the regularization term is a simple prior on the image. This makes Tikhonov a useful bridge: it is elementary enough to derive completely, but it already contains the logic behind many modern reconstruction methods.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Tikhonov Regularization
The simplest Tikhonov model is

x_\lambda
=
\operatorname*{argmin}_x
\|Ax-y\|_2^2+\lambda\|x\|_2^2.

The parameter \lambda>0 controls the strength of regularization.
The objective has two terms:







Term
Role




\|Ax-y\|_2^2
prefer reconstructions that explain the measurements


\lambda\|x\|_2^2
prefer smaller-energy solutions



If two images explain the data similarly, Tikhonov prefers the one with smaller norm.


Changing \lambda moves the reconstruction along a path from unstable to over-damped.

Very small \lambda leaves unstable directions barely damped. Moderate \lambda can control noise while preserving structure. Large \lambda over-damps the solution.
The phrase “smaller-energy solution” should be interpreted carefully. For a signal represented as a vector, \|x\|_2^2 penalizes large coefficients. For images, this can shrink contrast and suppress detail. It does not directly say “prefer sharp edges” or “prefer natural texture.” This is why basic Tikhonov is stable but often visually too smooth.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Probabilistic Interpretation
Tikhonov can also be read as a maximum a posteriori estimate. Suppose

y=Ax+\eta,
\qquad
\eta\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2I),

and suppose we place a Gaussian prior on x:

p(x)\propto \exp\left(-\frac{\lambda}{2\sigma^2}\|x\|_2^2\right).

Maximizing the posterior probability is equivalent to minimizing

\|Ax-y\|_2^2+\lambda\|x\|_2^2.

This interpretation is useful because it makes the modeling assumptions explicit. The data term says the measurement noise is Gaussian. The regularizer says smaller-energy images are more likely before seeing the data. Whether that prior is appropriate depends on the imaging task.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Deriving The Normal Equations
Let

J(x)=\|Ax-y\|_2^2+\lambda\|x\|_2^2.

Expanding,

J(x)=(Ax-y)^T(Ax-y)+\lambda x^T x.

The gradient is

\nabla J(x)=2A^T(Ax-y)+2\lambda x.

At a minimizer,

\nabla J(x_\lambda)=0.

Setting the gradient to zero gives

A^T(Ax_\lambda-y)+\lambda x_\lambda=0.

Therefore,

(A^TA+\lambda I)x_\lambda=A^Ty.

These are the Tikhonov normal equations.
The normal equations also show why \lambda stabilizes the computation. Even when A^TA has very small eigenvalues, the matrix A^TA+\lambda I has eigenvalues shifted by \lambda. This does not make the original measurement more informative, but it prevents the reconstruction from chasing directions where the data are nearly silent.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Closed-Form Solution
If \lambda>0,

x_\lambda
=
(A^TA+\lambda I)^{-1}A^Ty.

Tikhonov replaces an unstable inverse by a stabilized linear solve.
If A has singular values \sigma_i, then A^TA has eigenvalues \sigma_i^2. Tikhonov shifts them:

\sigma_i^2
\quad\longmapsto\quad
\sigma_i^2+\lambda.

Small eigenvalues are lifted away from zero.
For small educational examples, the direct matrix form is:
lhs = A.T @ A + lam * np.eye(n)
rhs = A.T @ y
x_lam = np.linalg.solve(lhs, rhs)
For large imaging problems, one usually avoids forming dense matrices explicitly and uses iterative methods or operator implementations.
This point is practical. A 512\times512 image has 262,144 pixels. The matrix for a general linear operator on that image would have more than 68 billion entries if stored densely. Imaging algorithms therefore usually apply A and A^T as functions: blur an image, subsample pixels, apply a Fourier transform, backproject, and so on.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
SVD Interpretation
Let

A v_i = \sigma_i u_i.

Then Tikhonov can be written as

x_\lambda
=
\sum_i
\frac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_i^2+\lambda}
\langle y,u_i\rangle v_i.

Compare this with direct inversion, whose factor is

\frac{1}{\sigma_i}.

Tikhonov replaces it with

\frac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_i^2+\lambda}.

When \sigma_i is small, Tikhonov avoids division by a tiny number.


Filter factors show how each singular direction is damped.

This plot is the SVD version of the reconstruction story. Direct inversion uses very large factors when \sigma_i is small. Tikhonov bends those factors down. The parameter \lambda decides where that bending begins.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Generalized Tikhonov
The basic penalty \|x\|_2^2 is not the only possibility. A common generalized form is

x_\lambda
=
\operatorname*{argmin}_x
\|Ax-y\|_2^2+\lambda\|Lx\|_2^2,

where L is another linear operator. If L is a finite-difference gradient, then the penalty discourages large variations. If L is a Laplacian, then the penalty discourages curvature.
This is still a quadratic model, but it is already more image-aware than penalizing the image intensity itself. It also previews later chapters: once we begin choosing penalties that better match image structure, we are designing priors.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Choosing Lambda
The parameter \lambda is the lesson.
Small \lambda gives better data fit but more noise amplification. Large \lambda gives more stability but more bias and blur.


The best parameter balances data fit and regularization.

The true image is usually unknown, so an RMSE curve is a teaching diagnostic rather than a practical parameter-choice method. In practice, parameter choice may use validation data, discrepancy principles, cross-validation, L-curve heuristics, or domain-specific performance criteria.


The visually best value of \lambda depends on the task and risk.

In a low-stakes photograph, a visually pleasing reconstruction may be enough. In medical imaging, a parameter that hides small structures can be unacceptable even if the image looks smoother.
When reading the image panel, do not search only for the prettiest image. Ask what changed as \lambda increased. Are noise oscillations reduced? Are edges softened? Are small features removed? The correct parameter depends on which errors matter for the application.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Bias And Variance
Imagine repeating the noisy measurement many times.
Bias is systematic error from the average reconstruction. Variance is random variation across noise realizations.


Regularization trades variance for bias.

Increasing \lambda usually reduces variance and increases bias. The best mean squared error often lies between the extremes.
The error can be large for two opposite reasons:

if \lambda is too small, noise amplification remains;
if \lambda is too large, the reconstruction is over-smoothed and biased.

This language will reappear when we discuss learned methods. A neural network can also reduce variance by restricting reconstructions to images that look familiar from training data. But that restriction can introduce bias: unusual structures may be suppressed because they are not well represented in the training set.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
What Tikhonov Does Not Solve
Tikhonov is a first regularization method, not the final answer to imaging.
It tends to prefer small-energy solutions, which may over-smooth edges or texture. It also depends on a linear model and a quadratic penalty in its basic form.
Later chapters introduce more expressive regularizers, including variational penalties, total variation, sparsity, wavelets, and learned priors.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
From Tikhonov To Learned Reconstruction
Tikhonov teaches a pattern that will survive even when the methods become more sophisticated:

\text{reconstruction}
=
\text{data consistency}
+
\text{image model}.

In Tikhonov, the image model is the simple penalty \|x\|_2^2. In total variation, the image model prefers piecewise-smooth images with edges. In sparse reconstruction, it prefers concise representations. In neural imaging, the image model may be learned from data.
The learned version is not conceptually unrelated to Tikhonov. It is a richer answer to the same question: how should the reconstruction behave in directions where the measurement is weak, ambiguous, or noisy?
This is why the course builds toward neural networks through inverse problems. Neural networks become meaningful when we understand what they are being asked to replace or augment: the prior, the optimizer, the inverse map, or some combination of these.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Common Mistakes
A first mistake is to think that increasing \lambda always improves the reconstruction. It improves stability, but it can also erase true structure.
A second mistake is to judge \lambda only by visual smoothness. Smooth images can be wrong. A reconstruction should be judged against the task, the data, and the measurement model.
A third mistake is to form large dense matrices because the formula contains A^TA. The formula is useful for analysis, but practical imaging code often works with operators.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Takeaways

Tikhonov solves \min_x \|Ax-y\|_2^2+\lambda\|x\|_2^2.
The normal equations are (A^TA+\lambda I)x=A^Ty.
In SVD coordinates, Tikhonov damps weak singular directions.
\lambda controls a bias-stability tradeoff.
Regularization is a modeling choice, not magic.
Neural reconstruction methods can be viewed as replacing or enriching simple regularizers with learned image models.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Why Noise Models Matter
In the first chapters we wrote idealized forward models such as blur or Fourier filtering. Real measurements also contain random variation.
A common observation model is

y = f(x) + \eta,

where x is the unknown scene or image, f is the forward imaging process, y is the recorded data, and \eta is noise.
When the forward model is linear, this becomes

y = Ax + \eta.

Linearity is a useful special case, not a universal law of imaging. Saturation, thresholding, occlusion, and many camera pipelines can be nonlinear or irreversible. The likelihood framework still applies if we replace Ax by a general prediction f(x).
This chapter changes the question slightly. Instead of asking only “what image could have produced the data?”, we ask “how probable are these data if the image were x?” That statistical question is the beginning of data fidelity.
This matters because reconstruction methods are not built from regularization alone. They usually contain two ingredients:

a data term, which tells us how seriously to take disagreement with the measurements;
a prior or regularizer, which tells us what kinds of images are plausible when the data are incomplete or unstable.

Noise models are how we derive the first ingredient. Without them, a cost function such as \|Ax-y\|_2^2 can look like a purely algebraic habit. With them, the same expression becomes a statement about the measurement process.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Seeing Noise Before Modeling It
The same clean image can be corrupted in visibly different ways. Additive Gaussian noise has a roughly brightness-independent texture. Photon-counting noise changes with the number of photons reaching each pixel.


Different noise models create different visual artifacts.

For a pixel i, the recorded value y_i is a random variable. A noise model specifies the probability law of y_i when the unknown true value is x_i.
A useful noise model answers:

what recorded values are likely;
how large typical errors are;
whether the variance depends on intensity;
what cost should penalize disagreement with data.

When you look at the figure, pay attention to two different effects. The Gaussian image has a similar grain in dark and bright regions. The Poisson image is more uneven in low-light regions because the number of photons there is small. The important point is not only that the images look different. The important point is that the same numerical error may have different statistical meanings in different parts of the image.
For example, an error of five gray levels may be large in a dark region and ordinary in a bright region under a photon-counting model. A good data term should know this.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Gaussian Noise
The simplest model is additive Gaussian noise:

y_i = x_i + \eta_i,
\qquad
\eta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2).

The parameter \sigma is the standard deviation of the noise. Under this model,

\mathbb{E}[y_i\mid x_i] = x_i,
\qquad
\mathrm{Var}(y_i\mid x_i)=\sigma^2.

The variance is constant: it does not depend on x_i.


Gaussian residuals are centered and symmetric, while Poisson counts change shape with intensity.

For one pixel,

p(y_i\mid x_i)
=
\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}
\exp\left(
-\frac{(y_i-x_i)^2}{2\sigma^2}
\right).

If pixel errors are independent, then

p(y\mid x)=\prod_i p(y_i\mid x_i).

Taking negative logarithms gives

-\log p(y\mid x)
=
\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_i (y_i-x_i)^2
+ \text{constant}.

Thus Gaussian noise leads naturally to least squares.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Gaussian Data Fidelity
For direct observation y=x+\eta, maximum likelihood gives

\operatorname*{argmin}_x
\frac{1}{2}\|y-x\|_2^2
= y.

For a linear forward model

y = Ax + \eta,
\qquad
\eta\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2 I),

the Gaussian negative log-likelihood is

\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\|Ax-y\|_2^2.

For a general nonlinear forward model

y = f(x)+\eta,

the corresponding Gaussian data term is

\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\|f(x)-y\|_2^2.

The squared error is therefore not just a convenient algebraic choice. It is the data fidelity term associated with a particular statistical assumption.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Gaussian Limitations
Gaussian noise is useful and mathematically friendly, but it is not always physically faithful. An additive Gaussian model can produce negative intensities or values above the sensor maximum. In practice we often clip values, but clipping changes the statistical model.
This is a general lesson: a simple model can be useful without being literally complete.
Gaussian models are still common for good reasons. They are often reasonable approximations when many independent small error sources are added together, and they lead to convex quadratic objectives that are easy to analyze and optimize. In this course, Gaussian noise is also the cleanest way to understand the bridge from probability to least squares.
The caution is that “least squares” should not become automatic. Before using it, ask what kind of measurement error is being assumed.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Poisson Noise
Light arrives as photons, and a sensor counts random arrivals during exposure. When photon counting is the dominant source of randomness, a Poisson model is natural.
Let z_i be the photon count at pixel i:

z_i \sim \operatorname{Poisson}(\lambda_i).

The Poisson law is

\mathbb{P}(z_i=k\mid \lambda_i)
= e^{-\lambda_i}\frac{\lambda_i^k}{k!}.

For a Poisson random variable,

\mathbb{E}[z_i]=\lambda_i,
\qquad
\mathrm{Var}(z_i)=\lambda_i.

The standard deviation is \sqrt{\lambda_i}. Absolute noise increases with brightness, but relative noise decreases:

\frac{\sqrt{\lambda_i}}{\lambda_i}
=
\frac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda_i}}.

This is why low-light imaging is difficult. A pixel receiving 5 photons is much less reliable, relatively speaking, than a pixel receiving 500 photons.


Signal-to-noise behavior depends strongly on the noise model.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
From Counts To Intensities
Digital images often store normalized intensities in [0,1]. If \alpha is the maximum expected photon count, a simple count model is

z_i \sim \operatorname{Poisson}(\alpha x_i),
\qquad
y_i = z_i/\alpha.

The normalized value y_i is still random. Increasing exposure increases \alpha, which improves relative precision when the model is valid.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Poisson Likelihood
For independent photon counts,

p(z\mid x)
=
\prod_i
e^{-\alpha x_i}
\frac{(\alpha x_i)^{z_i}}{z_i!}.

Ignoring constants independent of x,

-\log p(z\mid x)
=
\sum_i
\alpha x_i - z_i\log(\alpha x_i).

For a general positive forward model f_i(x)>0,

z_i \sim \operatorname{Poisson}(f_i(x))

and

-\log p(z\mid x)
=
\sum_i f_i(x) - z_i\log(f_i(x))
+ \text{constant}.

This is not a least-squares data term.
The positivity condition is not cosmetic. A Poisson mean must be nonnegative, and the logarithm requires a positive predicted count. In numerical code, this often forces us to constrain the reconstruction or to evaluate \log(f_i(x)+\epsilon) with a very small \epsilon. Such details are not just programming details; they are part of making the mathematical model usable.


Likelihood curves encode how each model penalizes disagreement with the data.

Reading this plot is an important skill. A likelihood curve is steep where the model says a mismatch is unlikely, and flatter where the model is more tolerant. For Gaussian noise with fixed variance, the curve is a symmetric parabola. For Poisson data, the curve is asymmetric because counts cannot be negative and because the variance changes with the mean.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Gaussian Versus Poisson



Feature
Gaussian
Poisson




Data type
continuous intensity
integer counts


Variance
constant \sigma^2
equals mean


Typical fidelity
squared error
count likelihood


Natural for
read noise, aggregate errors
photon-limited imaging


Constraint
often unconstrained
positive mean



The choice of data term should come from the measurement process. A squared error term may be appropriate for Gaussian read noise, but a photon-limited acquisition suggests a Poisson likelihood.
Real cameras may contain several noise sources at once. A simplified pipeline might include photon noise, electronic read noise, dark current, quantization, clipping, compression, and demosaicing. Modeling every part of that pipeline may be unnecessary for a first reconstruction method, but it is useful to know what has been simplified away.
This is one reason image restoration papers often specify both the degradation model and the noise model. “Denoising” by itself is not one problem. Denoising Gaussian noise, photon noise, JPEG artifacts, and sensor artifacts are related but not identical tasks.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Repeated Measurements
If

y^{(j)} = x + \eta^{(j)},
\qquad
\eta^{(j)}\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2),

and the noise samples are independent, then the average

\bar{y}=\frac{1}{m}\sum_{j=1}^m y^{(j)}

has noise standard deviation \sigma/\sqrt{m}.


Independent random noise can average out.

Averaging cannot recover information that never reached the sensor. If scene content is physically hidden behind an occluder, repeated photographs from the same viewpoint do not reveal the hidden object. This is different from reducing random fluctuations in recorded pixels.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
From Likelihood To Reconstruction
Given data y, maximum likelihood estimates x by

\operatorname*{argmax}_x p(y\mid x),

or equivalently

\operatorname*{argmin}_x -\log p(y\mid x).

Maximum likelihood uses the data model, but it is often not enough. In inverse problems, many images may agree almost equally well with noisy data.
A common regularized reconstruction form is

\operatorname*{argmin}_x
D(y,f(x)) + \lambda R(x).

Here D is the data fidelity term from the noise model, and R is a regularizer encoding prior information or desired structure.
This formula is one of the main templates of the course. The data term asks the reconstruction to be faithful to the measurement process. The regularizer asks it to be a plausible image. Different imaging methods can often be compared by asking two questions:

What is the data term?
What is the image model or prior?

Classical inverse problems usually choose R explicitly: smoothness, small energy, total variation, sparsity, or another mathematical prior. Neural methods often learn part of this structure from examples. They may learn a direct map from y to x, learn a denoiser used inside an algorithm, or learn a regularizer implicitly through training.
The likelihood still matters in learned methods. A neural reconstruction that ignores the measurement model may produce visually convincing images that are not faithful to the data. In imaging, visual plausibility and measurement consistency are not the same thing.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Common Mistakes
A first mistake is to treat all noise as Gaussian because squared error is familiar. Squared error is a model choice. Sometimes it is a good one, sometimes it is only a convenient approximation, and sometimes it is misleading.
A second mistake is to confuse random noise with missing information. Random noise can sometimes be reduced by repeated measurements or averaging. Missing scene content, physical occlusion, or unobserved frequencies require additional assumptions; averaging does not create information that was never measured.
A third mistake is to think that a lower data term always means a better image. With noisy data, fitting the measurements perfectly can mean fitting noise. This is why likelihood must later be balanced with regularization.

text:
Computation

python3 examples/week04_noise_likelihood.py
or open the notebook in Colab from the link at the top of this chapter.

text:
Exercises

For y_i=x_i+\eta_i with \eta_i\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2), compute \mathbb{E}[y_i\mid x_i] and \mathrm{Var}(y_i\mid x_i).
For a Poisson pixel with expected count \lambda=25, compute the standard deviation and approximate SNR.

In your own words, explain why a neural reconstruction method should still care about the measurement noise model.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Takeaways

Noise models connect physical measurement assumptions to mathematical data terms.
Gaussian additive noise leads to squared-error fidelity.
Poisson photon-counting noise has variance equal to its mean.
Low-light images are difficult because relative photon noise is high.
Likelihood terms are only one part of reconstruction; regularization is needed when the inverse problem is ambiguous or unstable.
Learned imaging methods still need data consistency; plausibility alone is not enough.

title: Convolution and Blur
text: Slides | Notebook | Open in Colab

title: Convolution and Blur
Convolution and Blur
section: Why Blur Is The First Model
text:
Why Blur Is The First Model
Blur is one of the cleanest examples of the course philosophy. It is simple enough to write down, visible enough to understand by looking at images, and serious enough to reveal the main difficulty of inverse problems.
In a blurred image, information has not simply disappeared at random. It has been mixed. A bright point spreads into nearby pixels. An edge becomes a transition zone. Fine texture becomes weaker. If the blur is known, we can write a forward model. But going backward remains unstable because the blur has reduced the contrast of the very details we may want to recover.
This makes blur a good bridge between the first chapter and the rest of the course:

\text{image } x
\quad\longrightarrow\quad
\text{blurred data } y
\quad\longrightarrow\quad
\text{reconstruction } \hat{x}.

The first arrow is image formation. The second arrow is the inverse problem.

title: Convolution and Blur
text:
Local Weighted Averages
Convolution begins with a local rule. For a one-dimensional signal x, define

y_i = \frac{1}{4}x_{i-1}+\frac{1}{2}x_i+\frac{1}{4}x_{i+1}.

The output at index i is a weighted average of nearby input values. The weights

h =
\begin{bmatrix}
\frac{1}{4} & \frac{1}{2} & \frac{1}{4}
\end{bmatrix}

form a convolution kernel.
If the weights are nonnegative and sum to 1, the operation preserves average brightness for constant signals.


Convolution spreads a local peak into neighboring positions.

The figure should be read from left to right. The original signal contains a localized structure. The kernel says how each value is distributed to its neighbors. The output is less sharp because information from nearby locations has been averaged together.
This is not just smoothing for visual effect. It is a measurement model. If an optical system cannot record a point exactly, then every recorded pixel contains contributions from a neighborhood of the object.

title: Convolution and Blur
text:
The Convolution Formula
One common discrete convention is

(h*x)_i = \sum_k h_k x_{i-k}.

For symmetric kernels, convolution and correlation produce the same result. For nonsymmetric kernels, the reversal in the index convention matters.
For a fixed kernel h, convolution is linear:

h*(a x + b z) = a(h*x)+b(h*z).

It is also shift-invariant: the same local rule is applied at every position.
Linearity means that if we double the input intensity, the output doubles, and if we add two inputs, the outputs add. Shift-invariance means that the rule does not depend on where we are in the image. Together, these assumptions make convolution mathematically convenient.
They are also assumptions. Real imaging systems may have spatially varying blur, saturation, nonlinear sensor response, or motion that changes during acquisition. We begin with convolution because it is the right model in many controlled situations and because it teaches the structure of inverse problems clearly.

title: Convolution and Blur
text:
Two-Dimensional Convolution
For an image x[i,j] and a 2D kernel h[a,b],

y[i,j]
=
(h*x)[i,j]
=
\sum_{a,b} h[a,b]\,x[i-a,j-b].

The output pixel is a weighted combination of nearby input pixels.
The two-dimensional formula looks more complicated than the one-dimensional formula, but the idea is the same. Each output pixel is a local weighted average. What changes is the geometry of the neighborhood: the kernel now has horizontal and vertical extent, so it can model circular blur, directional blur, or other spatial spreading patterns.

title: Convolution and Blur
text:
Point Spread Function
The point spread function (PSF) describes how an imaging system records a single ideal bright point.
If a point is recorded as a small Gaussian blob, then the image is blurred by approximately the same Gaussian pattern at every location. Under that assumption, the imaging model is convolution.


Common kernels model different kinds of blur.




Kernel
Effect
Possible interpretation




identity
no blur
ideal sampling


box average
local averaging
simple smoothing


Gaussian
smooth blur
defocus or optics


motion
directional streak
camera or object motion



The identity kernel is useful as a reference: it represents a system that records each pixel without spreading. A box kernel says that all pixels in a small neighborhood contribute equally. A Gaussian kernel gives more weight to the center and less to farther pixels. A motion kernel spreads energy along a direction.
Each kernel tells a story about the imaging system. The inverse problem should be interpreted relative to that story. If the wrong kernel is used, a deblurring method may confidently solve the wrong problem.

title: Convolution and Blur
text:
Blur As A Forward Operator
If h is fixed, then blur can be written as

y = h*x.

After vectorization, this becomes

y = Ax,

where A is the matrix that represents convolution by h under the chosen boundary convention.






Note



A fixed blur is linear. This does not mean every image formation process is linear. It means this particular controlled model is linear.


The matrix A is usually not formed explicitly for large images. It would be enormous. Instead, software applies the convolution rule directly. Still, the matrix viewpoint is valuable because it lets us use linear algebra words: range, nullspace, singular values, conditioning, and adjoint.
This is a recurring pattern in computational imaging. We may never build the full matrix, but we still reason as if an operator is present.

title: Convolution and Blur
text:
Real Image Blur


Different kernels remove different details from the same image.

Blur reduces local contrast. Edges become less sharp, fine textures disappear, and point-like structures spread into neighborhoods.
When reading blurred images, look separately at three kinds of information:

strong edges, which may survive but become wider;
fine texture, which may almost disappear;
small bright or dark structures, which may spread and lose contrast.

These distinctions matter later. A method that improves edge sharpness may still destroy fine texture. A method that makes the image visually pleasing may still remove a small structure that matters for a task.

title: Convolution and Blur
text:
Boundary Conditions
Near the edge of an image, a convolution kernel asks for values outside the recorded grid. Boundary conditions decide what those values mean.
Common choices include:



Boundary condition
Meaning




zero padding
outside pixels are zero


reflection
image reflects at the boundary


periodic
image wraps around


nearest
outside pixels copy the nearest boundary value





The same kernel can produce different edge behavior depending on the boundary rule.

Boundary choices are part of the mathematical model. They should not be treated as harmless implementation details.
Boundary conditions are easy to ignore because they live at the edge of the image, but they can affect computations throughout an inverse problem. A periodic boundary condition, for example, pretends that the left and right sides of the image touch. That may be convenient for Fourier analysis, but it can create artificial wrap-around artifacts.
In project work, a good report should state the boundary convention when convolution or deconvolution is used.

title: Convolution and Blur
text:
Why Deblurring Is Hard
Deblurring asks for an image x from a blurred observation y:

y = Ax + \eta.

If blur attenuates fine details, then trying to invert the blur must amplify the components that were weakened. Unfortunately, noise is also amplified in those same directions.


Naive inversion can turn small noise into large oscillations.

This is the first appearance of a central course theme: reconstruction is not only inversion. It is stabilized inversion.
The unstable oscillations in the figure are not mysterious. They are the visible form of amplified weak information. Blur has made some components of the signal small. Naive inversion divides by small numbers, and noise becomes large.
Regularization will solve this by refusing to trust every inverted component equally. A neural reconstruction method may solve it differently, by learning from examples what sharp images tend to look like. But the danger is the same in both cases: the method may add structure that is not strongly supported by the data.

title: Convolution and Blur
text:
Common Mistakes

Thinking blur only removes information. Blur also mixes information spatially. That mixing can sometimes be partially undone, but not stably without assumptions.
Confusing the kernel with the image. The kernel describes the imaging system, not the object being imaged.
Ignoring boundary conditions. Boundary rules are part of the operator A.
Assuming deblurring is just sharpening. Sharpening may improve appearance, but deblurring is an inverse problem tied to a forward model.

text:
Computation

When using the notebook, compare two questions:

What happens visually when the blur kernel gets wider?
What happens numerically when the deblurring stabilizer gets weaker?

The first question is about the forward problem. The second is about the inverse problem.

text:
Exercises

Explain why a normalized blur kernel preserves constant images.
Compare zero-padding and reflection near an image boundary.

title: Convolution and Blur
text:
Takeaways

Convolution is a local weighted averaging rule.
A point spread function models how one ideal point is spread by the imaging system.
Fixed blur is linear and can be written as y=Ax.
Boundary conditions are modeling choices.
Deblurring is unstable because blur weakens information that inversion tries to recover.

title: Motivation
text:
This course is about images, but it is not mainly about displaying images. It is about recovering, interpreting, and trusting images from imperfect data.
The central question is:

Given measurements produced by an imaging system, what can we infer about the image or object that produced them?

This is an inverse problem. The data are not the object itself. The data are the result of a measurement process.
In its simplest form, we write

y = A x + \eta.

Here x is the unknown image or object, A is the forward model, y is the measured data, and \eta represents noise or error. The forward model tells us how an image would produce data. The inverse problem asks us to go in the opposite direction.
That opposite direction is usually difficult. Information may have been blurred, sampled, hidden, corrupted, or compressed. Some information may be weakened. Some may be missing entirely. A reconstruction method must therefore do more than invert a formula. It must use assumptions.

The central question is:

Given measurements produced by an imaging system, what can we infer about the image or object that produced them?

This is an inverse problem. The data are not the object itself. The data are the result of a measurement process.
In its simplest form, we write

y = A x + \eta.

Here x is the unknown image or object, A is the forward model, y is the measured data, and \eta represents noise or error. The forward model tells us how an image would produce data. The inverse problem asks us to go in the opposite direction.
That opposite direction is usually difficult. Information may have been blurred, sampled, hidden, corrupted, or compressed. Some information may be weakened. Some may be missing entirely. A reconstruction method must therefore do more than invert a formula. It must use assumptions.
crumbs: Motivation: inverse problems to neural networks

title: Motivation
text:
Why Images Are Different
Images are not just long signals. They are spatial objects. A pixel has neighbors. Edges have geometry. Textures have scale and orientation. Objects can occlude other objects. Small structures may matter.
This makes imaging richer than many one-dimensional signal problems. It also makes imaging more dangerous. A reconstruction can look visually plausible while being wrong in a way that matters.
For example, if pixels are missing from an already recorded image, we can model that as a mask and try to fill in the missing values. But if a person is physically hidden behind a tree, the camera did not measure the hidden part. A method may guess what could be behind the tree, but that guess is not recovered evidence. It is an assumption.
This distinction is one reason the course returns repeatedly to the same question:

Which part of the result comes from the data, and which part comes from the model?
crumbs: Motivation: inverse problems to neural networks

title: Motivation
text:
Classical Reconstruction
Classical mathematical imaging often solves an optimization problem:

\hat{x}
=
\arg\min_x
D(Ax,y)+\lambda R(x).

The term D(Ax,y) measures agreement with the data. It asks whether the reconstructed image, when passed through the forward model, produces measurements close to y.
The term R(x) expresses prior knowledge. It may prefer smooth images, sparse coefficients, small gradients, or piecewise-constant regions. The parameter \lambda controls the balance between fitting the data and trusting the prior.
This formula is not only a computational recipe. It is a statement of responsibility. Every reconstruction has assumptions, and those assumptions must be made visible.
crumbs: Motivation: inverse problems to neural networks

title: Motivation
text:
The Move Toward Neural Networks
Neural networks enter naturally once the course has developed this language.
Instead of hand-designing a prior R(x), we may try to learn prior knowledge from examples. Instead of designing every step of an iterative algorithm, we may learn part of the reconstruction rule. Instead of writing a closed-form regularizer, we may use a trained denoiser inside an algorithm.
This gives several views of neural reconstruction:








View
Mathematical form
Interpretation




learned inverse map
\hat{x}=f_\theta(y)
learn the reconstruction directly from data


learned prior
R_\theta(x)
learn what plausible images look like


learned denoiser
D_\theta(z)
use a network as an image-cleaning operator


unrolled algorithm
x^{k+1}=F_{\theta,k}(x^k,y)
learn parts of an iterative solver


plug-and-play method
x^{k+1}=D_\theta(x^k-\tau A^\top(Ax^k-y))
combine data consistency with a learned denoiser



This is why the course does not treat neural networks as a separate topic added at the end. Neural networks are one answer to questions that arise naturally from inverse problems:

What assumptions should we impose on unknown images?
Can those assumptions be learned from data?
How do we keep reconstructions consistent with measurements?
How do we detect hallucinations or failures?
How do we evaluate reliability under distribution shift?

title: Motivation
text:
What This Course Is Not
This course is not a general course on deep learning. It does not begin with architectures, optimizers, and benchmark datasets.
Instead, it asks what neural networks mean in an imaging context. A network that reconstructs an image is part of an inverse problem, explicitly or implicitly. It may replace an inverse operator, approximate a prior, act as a denoiser, or imitate an optimization algorithm.
That perspective changes the questions we ask. We do not only ask whether the output looks good. We ask:

What forward model is being inverted?
What data were measured?
What information was lost?
What prior knowledge was used?
Was the prior hand-designed or learned?
Is the output data-consistent?
What happens under noise, model mismatch, or domain shift?
crumbs: Motivation: inverse problems to neural networks

title: Motivation
section: How The Book Develops The Idea
text:
How The Book Develops The Idea
The book follows the course arc.
Chapters 1-3 build the foundation: images as mathematical objects, blur as a forward model, and Fourier analysis as a way to see what blur removes.
Chapters 4-6 introduce noise, likelihoods, ill-posedness, and regularization. These chapters explain why reconstruction requires assumptions.
Chapters 7-9 show how assumptions become variational problems and numerical algorithms.
Chapters 10-13 introduce sparsity, wavelets, data-driven priors, and plug-and-play methods. This is where the bridge to neural reconstruction becomes explicit.
Chapter 14 asks how to trust any reconstruction, classical or learned.
The final project asks students to use these ideas in a concrete inverse problem, with LLM assistance required but ownership tested through experiments and oral defense.
crumbs: Motivation: inverse problems to neural networks

title: Motivation
text:
The Habit Of Mind
The most important habit in the course is to read a reconstruction as an argument.
A reconstructed image argues that a certain unknown x is compatible with measured data y, a forward model A, a noise model, and a set of assumptions. The image may be beautiful, but beauty is not evidence by itself.
The course therefore trains two instincts at the same time:

how to build reconstruction methods;
how to question reconstruction methods.

That double instinct is essential for modern imaging. It is also essential for neural networks, because learned reconstructions can be powerful precisely where they can also be misleading.

title: MATH 435: Mathematical Imaging
text:
MATH 435 studies images through one central question:
The course begins with classical mathematical imaging: images as arrays and functions, convolution, Fourier analysis, noise, ill-posedness, regularization, variational models, sparsity, and wavelets. These ideas naturally lead to modern neural reconstruction methods. In this course, neural networks are not introduced as magic black boxes. They appear as learned inverse maps, learned priors, learned denoisers, or learned components inside reconstruction algorithms.
The guiding progression is:
y = Ax+\eta
\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
\hat{x}
=
\arg\min_x D(Ax,y)+\lambda R(x)
\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
\hat{x}=f_\theta(y)
and to hybrid methods such as plug-and-play reconstruction, where the forward model is still used but part of the prior knowledge is learned from data.

title: MATH 435: Mathematical Imaging
text:
Central Idea
Classical imaging asks:

What assumptions should we impose on the unknown image?

Neural imaging asks:

Can those assumptions, or the reconstruction algorithm itself, be learned from examples?

The course treats both questions as part of the same mathematical story. Every method, classical or learned, must still be judged by its forward model, its assumptions, its stability, its failures, and its consequences.

title: Syllabus
text:
Course Catalog Description
This course develops the mathematical foundations of imaging science from a modern inverse problems perspective. Students learn how images are formed, degraded, and reconstructed using tools from linear algebra, Fourier analysis, probability, variational modeling, and optimization.
The course emphasizes mathematical modeling, stability analysis, regularization techniques (Tikhonov, Total Variation, sparse methods), and computational implementation. Classical model-based approaches are connected to modern computational imaging techniques, including data-driven and learning-based reconstruction methods.
Applications focus on medical and scientific imaging.

title: Mathematical Imaging
Images as Mathematical Objects
text: Slides | Notebook | Open in Colab

title: Images as Mathematical Objects
Images as Mathematical Objects
text:
Why This Chapter Matters
The first step in an inverse problem is to decide what the unknown is. In this course, the unknown is usually an image, but the word “image” can mean several things: a physical scene, a continuous intensity function, a digital array, a vector of numbers, or a displayed picture.
Those meanings are connected, but they are not identical. A camera does not record a mathematical function directly. It records finite measurements. A display does not show an abstract vector directly. It turns numbers into light. A reconstruction algorithm works with arrays and vectors, even when we interpret the output as a scene, an object, or a medical image.
This chapter establishes the basic habit of the course:

separate the object we want to know from the data we actually have.

That separation is what makes inverse problems possible to formulate. It is also what later allows us to understand neural networks as maps from measured data to reconstructed images.

title: Images as Mathematical Objects
text:
Images As Functions
At the mathematical level, a grayscale image is often modeled as a function

u : \Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}.

The domain \Omega is the region being imaged. The value u(s,t) is the intensity at location (s,t). This continuous model is useful because many imaging systems are described physically before they are discretized.
For a color image, the value has several components:

u : \Omega \to \mathbb{R}^3.

For a video, a 3D volume, or a multispectral image, the range and the domain can have even more dimensions. The same principle remains: an image is data organized over space, and sometimes over time, wavelength, or slice index.
The continuous model is an idealization. It lets us talk about geometry, edges, derivatives, integrals, and physical image formation. A digital image, by contrast, is finite. It has a limited number of pixels and a limited numerical precision. The two views are not competitors. The continuous view helps us formulate models; the discrete view lets us compute.
Many mathematical imaging methods quietly move between these two views. For example, a smoothness assumption may be written using derivatives of a continuous function, then implemented using finite differences on an array. Good modeling means remembering which level we are working at.

title: Images as Mathematical Objects
text:
Images As Arrays
A digital image is sampled on a grid. If the image has m rows and n columns, we write

U =
\begin{bmatrix}
u_{1,1} & u_{1,2} & \cdots & u_{1,n}\\
u_{2,1} & u_{2,2} & \cdots & u_{2,n}\\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots\\
u_{m,1} & u_{m,2} & \cdots & u_{m,n}
\end{bmatrix}.

The array is the natural form for display and local operations. Neighboring entries in the array correspond to neighboring pixels.






Note



The displayed photograph is not the mathematical object by itself. The mathematical object is the numerical array that the display visualizes.




Real image data can be inspected as an array, a pixel crop, or a vector.

When reading this figure, notice the change of scale. At normal viewing size, the photograph looks like a continuous visual object. After zooming, the pixel grid becomes visible. After vectorization, the same data no longer looks like an image at all, but the numerical information is still present.
This is a recurring tension in the course. Humans interpret images spatially. Algorithms often manipulate numbers algebraically. A good reconstruction method must respect both views: it must compute with arrays or vectors, but it must preserve structures that matter visually and scientifically.

title: Images as Mathematical Objects
text:
Images As Vectors
For linear algebra, we often reshape the image matrix U into a vector

x = \operatorname{vec}(U) \in \mathbb{R}^{mn}.

If row-major order is used, the rows of U are read from left to right:

U =
\begin{bmatrix}
10 & 20 & 30\\
40 & 50 & 60
\end{bmatrix}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\operatorname{vec}(U)=
\begin{bmatrix}
10 & 20 & 30 & 40 & 50 & 60
\end{bmatrix}^{T}.

Vectorization does not change the information. It changes the shape so that matrix-vector notation can be used.
Vectorization is powerful because it allows us to write large imaging operations as matrix-vector products. A blur, a mask, a Fourier sampling operator, or a projection can all be treated as operators acting on x.
Vectorization also hides spatial structure. Two pixels that are neighbors in the image may not look special as entries in a long vector unless the operator or regularizer remembers the grid. This is why later chapters will introduce operators such as gradients, wavelets, and convolution kernels. They encode spatial relationships that the raw vector notation does not show by itself.






Note



Vector notation is a language for computation, not a denial that images have geometry.

title: Images as Mathematical Objects
text:
Forward Models
Once the unknown image is written as a vector x, a controlled measurement process can often be written as

y = Ax + \eta.

Here:

x is the unknown image;
A is the forward operator;
y is the recorded data;
\eta is noise or measurement error.

This formula is a modeling template, not a claim that every visual process is linear. It is useful when the imaging system is controlled and the measurement rule is known well enough to write down.
Examples of forward operators include:



Operator
What it does




identity
records every pixel directly


mask
records only selected pixels


blur
spreads each pixel to its neighbors


Fourier sampling
records frequency coefficients


projection
records line integrals or summaries



The forward model points in the direction of measurement: from image to data. Reconstruction points in the opposite direction: from data back to an image estimate. The rest of the course is largely about why the reverse direction is harder.
It is tempting to think of A^{-1} as the goal. But in imaging, the forward operator may not be invertible, may be badly conditioned, or may not capture the full physical scene. Even when A is linear, the inverse problem may require assumptions about what images are likely. Those assumptions later appear as regularizers, priors, learned denoisers, and neural networks.
In neural-network language, a learned reconstruction map

\hat{x}=f_\theta(y)

can be viewed as a learned attempt to go from measured data to an image. The question remains the same: what has the method learned, and what evidence in y supports the output?

title: Images as Mathematical Objects
text:
Masking Recorded Pixels
A binary mask models which recorded pixels are available. If M selects a subset of entries of x, then

y = Mx

records only those entries.


Masking recorded pixels creates missing entries in the digital observation.

The inverse problem is then to infer something about the missing values from the observed ones and from assumptions about images.
The word “infer” is important. If a missing pixel is surrounded by similar pixels, interpolation may be reasonable. If a missing region crosses an edge, a smooth interpolation may be wrong. If a missing region is large, there may be many plausible completions. The data alone do not choose among them.
This is the first small example of a prior. A prior is a preference or assumption about the unknown image. It may be as simple as “nearby pixels are similar” or as complex as a neural network trained on many examples.

title: Images as Mathematical Objects
text:
Missing Pixels Versus Occlusion
It is important to distinguish two situations:

A pixel was recorded but then removed or masked.
A part of the physical scene was hidden from the sensor.

The first case is a missing-data problem in the recorded image. The second is a visibility problem in the physical scene.
If a person is behind a tree, the camera did not record the hidden part of the person. No mathematical method can recover information that was never measured without adding assumptions from outside the measurement. This is one reason image inverse problems are different from many idealized signal separation problems.
This distinction will matter again when we discuss hallucination. A reconstruction method can produce a plausible face, vessel, texture, or object part. Plausibility is not the same as measurement. In high-stakes imaging, the difference between “recovered from data” and “guessed from prior knowledge” must be visible.

title: Images as Mathematical Objects
text:
Common Mistakes

Confusing the display with the data. A displayed image is a visualization of numbers. Changing contrast or colormap changes the display, not necessarily the underlying data.
Thinking vectorization destroys the image. Vectorization changes the ordering of values, but the original array can be recovered if the shape and ordering are known.
Assuming every imaging process is linear. Some useful models are linear, but physical image formation can include occlusion, saturation, motion, nonlinear sensors, and other effects.
Treating missing pixels and hidden scene content as the same problem. Missing entries in an array are not the same as unobserved physical reality.

What is the unknown image x?
What is the observed data y?
What assumption would be needed to reconstruct what is missing?

Takeaways

An image can be viewed as a function, an array, or a vector.
Vectorization lets us write imaging models using linear algebra.
