title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Bridge from Week 3
Last time we wrote a noisy deblurring model as

\widehat{y} = \widehat{h}\widehat{x} + \widehat{\eta}.


We discussed why deblurring amplifies noise. Today: what is that noise?

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Observation Model
In imaging, we often write

y = f(x) + \eta.


x is the unknown scene or image, f is the forward imaging process, y is the recorded data, and \eta is random noise.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Linear Is a Special Case
When the forward model is linear, we can write

y = A x + \eta.


But not every imaging process is linear. Occlusion, saturation, thresholding, and many camera pipelines can break linearity.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Why Noise Models Matter



Uncertainty

How much should we trust a measurement?



Fidelity

Which reconstructions agree with data?



Algorithms

What objective should we minimize?

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Part 1: Seeing Noise

Before formulas

Look at the image

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text: Same Image, Different Noise

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
First Discussion

4 minutes


Compare the three noisy images.

Which noise looks independent of brightness?
Which noise looks worse in dark or low-count regions?
Which model seems closer to low-light photography?

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Random Variables at Pixels
For a pixel i, the recorded value y_i is a random variable.

The noise model specifies the probability law of y_i when the unknown true value is x_i.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
section: What We Want from a Model
text:
What We Want from a Model
A useful noise model should answer:


What values of y_i are likely?
How large are typical errors?
Does the variance depend on intensity?
What cost should penalize disagreement with data?

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Part 2: Gaussian Noise

Additive errors

Constant variance

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Gaussian Model
The simplest model is

y_i = x_i + \eta_i,
\qquad
\eta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2).


The parameter \sigma is the standard deviation of the noise.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Gaussian Assumptions


Errors are additive.
Errors have mean zero.
Errors are often assumed independent.
Error variance is the same for all pixels.



These assumptions are useful, not universal.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text: Gaussian Residuals

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Activity 1: Constant Variance

5 minutes


If y_i=x_i+\eta_i and \eta_i\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2), what are

\mathbb{E}[y_i\mid x_i]
\quad\text{and}\quad
\mathrm{Var}(y_i\mid x_i)?

Does the variance depend on x_i?

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Gaussian Density
For one pixel:

p(y_i\mid x_i)
=
\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}
\exp\left(
-\frac{(y_i-x_i)^2}{2\sigma^2}
\right).


The most likely value is x_i=y_i.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Independent Pixels
If the pixel errors are independent,

p(y\mid x)=
\prod_i p(y_i\mid x_i).

Taking negative logarithms gives

-\log p(y\mid x)
=
\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_i (y_i-x_i)^2
+ \text{constant}.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Gaussian Data Fidelity
For the direct observation model y=x+\eta:

\operatorname*{argmin}_x
\frac{1}{2}\|y-x\|_2^2
= y.


Gaussian noise leads naturally to least squares.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
With a Linear Forward Model
If the forward model is linear:

y = A x + \eta,
\qquad
\eta\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2 I),

then the Gaussian negative log-likelihood is

\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\|Ax-y\|_2^2.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
With a General Forward Model
If the forward model is nonlinear:

y = f(x)+\eta,

the Gaussian data term becomes

\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\|f(x)-y\|_2^2.


The likelihood idea does not require linearity.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Python Demo: Gaussian Noise

import numpy as np
from skimage import data

image = data.camera().astype(float) / 255
rng = np.random.default_rng(43504)

sigma = 0.08
noisy = np.clip(image + sigma * rng.standard_normal(image.shape), 0, 1)
residual = noisy - image

print(residual.mean(), residual.std())

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Gaussian Limitations

Can an additive Gaussian model produce negative pixel intensities or values above the sensor maximum?


In practice we often clip values. Clipping changes the statistical model.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Part 3: Poisson Noise

Photon counting

Variance depends on signal

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Why Poisson?
Light arrives as photons.
A sensor counts random arrivals during exposure.

Poisson noise is a natural model when the dominant randomness is photon counting.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Poisson Model
Let z_i be the photon count at pixel i.

z_i \sim \operatorname{Poisson}(\lambda_i).

The Poisson law is

\mathbb{P}(z_i=k\mid \lambda_i)
= e^{-\lambda_i}\frac{\lambda_i^k}{k!}.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Mean Equals Variance
For a Poisson random variable:

\mathbb{E}[z_i]=\lambda_i,
\qquad
\mathrm{Var}(z_i)=\lambda_i.


The standard deviation is \sqrt{\lambda_i}, so absolute noise increases with brightness.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Relative Noise
The relative fluctuation is roughly

\frac{\sqrt{\lambda_i}}{\lambda_i}
=
\frac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda_i}}.


More photons means better relative precision.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text: SNR Picture

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Activity 2: Photon Counts

5 minutes


A pixel has expected count \lambda=25.

What is the standard deviation?
What is the approximate SNR?
What happens to the SNR if exposure gives \lambda=100?

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Intensity Scaling
Digital images often store normalized intensities in [0,1].
If \alpha is the maximum expected photon count, a simple model is

z_i \sim \operatorname{Poisson}(\alpha x_i),
\qquad
y_i = z_i / \alpha.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Low Light Is Hard

Which is more reliable: observing 5 photons or 500 photons?


Low-light imaging is difficult because the data are made of small random counts.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Poisson Likelihood
For independent counts:

p(z\mid x)
=
\prod_i
e^{-\alpha x_i}
\frac{(\alpha x_i)^{z_i}}{z_i!}.

Ignoring constants independent of x:

-\log p(z\mid x)
=
\sum_i
\alpha x_i - z_i\log(\alpha x_i).

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Poisson Data Fidelity
For a general positive forward model f_i(x)>0:

z_i \sim \operatorname{Poisson}(f_i(x)).

Then

-\log p(z\mid x)
=
\sum_i f_i(x) - z_i\log(f_i(x))
+ \text{constant}.


This is not a least-squares data term.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text: Likelihood Curves

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Activity 3: Compare Costs

6 minutes


For one pixel, compare:

\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}(y-x)^2
\quad\text{and}\quad
\alpha x - z\log(\alpha x).

Which one is symmetric around its minimizer? Which one requires x>0?

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Gaussian vs Poisson



Feature
Gaussian
Poisson




Data type
continuous intensity
integer counts


Variance
constant \sigma^2
equals mean


Typical fidelity
squared error
count likelihood


Natural for
read noise, aggregate errors
photon-limited imaging


Constraint
often unconstrained
positive mean

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Part 4: Repeated Measurements

Noise can average out

Independence matters

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Averaging Gaussian Noise
If

y^{(j)} = x + \eta^{(j)},
\qquad
\eta^{(j)}\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2),

and the noise samples are independent, then

\bar{y}=\frac{1}{m}\sum_{j=1}^m y^{(j)}

has noise standard deviation \sigma/\sqrt{m}.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text: Averaging Picture

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
What Averaging Cannot Do

If the person is hidden behind a tree, can averaging many photographs reveal the hidden person?


Averaging reduces random noise in recorded data. It does not recover scene content that never reached the sensor.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Part 5: From Likelihood to Reconstruction

The inverse problem bridge

Data term plus prior

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Maximum Likelihood
Given data y, maximum likelihood estimates x by

\operatorname*{argmax}_x p(y\mid x).

Equivalently,

\operatorname*{argmin}_x -\log p(y\mid x).

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
section: Why Maximum Likelihood Is Not Enough
text:
Why Maximum Likelihood Is Not Enough

If many images agree almost equally well with the noisy data, how should we choose among them?


This is where regularization enters.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Regularized Reconstruction
A common form is

\operatorname*{argmin}_x
D(y, f(x)) + \lambda R(x).



D is the data fidelity term from the noise model.


R is a regularizer encoding prior information.

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Examples of Data Terms


Gaussian:

D(y,f(x))=
\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\|y-f(x)\|_2^2.



Poisson:

D(z,f(x))=
\sum_i f_i(x)-z_i\log f_i(x).

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
Suggested Answers







Question
Short answer




Gaussian to least squares
the negative log of a Gaussian density is quadratic


Poisson signal dependence
mean and variance are both \lambda


SNR
signal strength divided by typical noise size


likelihood role
it defines the data fidelity term

title: Noise Models and Likelihoods
text:
What Students Should Remember


A noise model is a probability law for measurements.
Gaussian noise has constant variance and leads to least squares.
Poisson noise models counts and has variance equal to the mean.
Likelihoods turn measurement assumptions into reconstruction objectives.
Regularization is still needed when data alone do not determine a stable image.

text:
Opening Question

Stabilizing an inverse

If exact inversion is unstable, what should we give up: exact data fit, sharpness, or both?

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Today

Derive the normal equations.
Interpret the regularization parameter.
Read Tikhonov filter factors.
Explain the bias-stability tradeoff.

Time
Work

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Bridge from Week 5
Week 5 showed that direct inversion may amplify noise:

\frac{\langle \eta,u_i\rangle}{\sigma_i}.


How can we keep useful directions without exploding in unstable directions?

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
The Basic Model
For a linear imaging model:

y = Ax + \eta.

The least-squares data fit is

\|Ax-y\|_2^2.


For Gaussian noise, this comes from the likelihood.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
The Problem with Least Squares
If A is ill-conditioned, the least-squares solution can fit noise.

Data fit alone does not know which data components are reliable.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Part 1: Tikhonov Model

Add a stabilizing penalty

Least squares plus size control

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Tikhonov Regularization
The simplest Tikhonov model is

x_\lambda
=
\operatorname*{argmin}_x
\|Ax-y\|_2^2 + \lambda\|x\|_2^2.


The parameter \lambda>0 controls the strength of regularization.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
What the Terms Mean


Data term:

\|Ax-y\|_2^2

prefer reconstructions that explain the measurements.


Penalty term:

\lambda\|x\|_2^2

prefer smaller-energy solutions.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Why Penalize the Solution?

If two images explain the data similarly, which one should we prefer?

Tikhonov’s answer:

Prefer the one with smaller norm.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Activity 1: Extreme Parameters

5 minutes


Predict what happens to

\|Ax-y\|_2^2+\lambda\|x\|_2^2

when:

\lambda \to 0;
\lambda \to \infty.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text: Parameter Path

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Reading the Path


Very small \lambda: unstable directions are barely damped.
Moderate \lambda: noise is controlled while structure remains.
Large \lambda: the solution is overly damped.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Part 2: Deriving the Normal Equations

Closed form

Differentiate the objective

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Objective Function
Let

J(x)=\|Ax-y\|_2^2+\lambda\|x\|_2^2.

Expanding:

J(x)=(Ax-y)^T(Ax-y)+\lambda x^T x.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Gradient
The gradient is

\nabla J(x)
=
2A^T(Ax-y)+2\lambda x.

At a minimizer:

\nabla J(x_\lambda)=0.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Normal Equations
Setting the gradient to zero gives:

A^T(Ax_\lambda-y)+\lambda x_\lambda=0.

Therefore:

(A^TA+\lambda I)x_\lambda=A^T y.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Closed-Form Solution
If \lambda>0,

x_\lambda
=
(A^TA+\lambda I)^{-1}A^T y.


Tikhonov replaces an unstable inverse by a stabilized linear solve.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Why This Is Better Conditioned
If A has singular values \sigma_i, then A^TA has eigenvalues \sigma_i^2.
Tikhonov shifts them:

\sigma_i^2
\quad\longmapsto\quad
\sigma_i^2+\lambda.


Small eigenvalues are lifted away from zero.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Activity 2: Condition Number

5 minutes


Suppose singular values are

1,\;0.1,\;0.001.

Compare the smallest eigenvalue of A^TA with that of A^TA+\lambda I for \lambda=0.01.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Python Demo: Normal Equations

lhs = A.T @ A + lam * np.eye(n)
rhs = A.T @ y
x_lam = np.linalg.solve(lhs, rhs)


This is the direct matrix form for small educational examples.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Part 3: SVD Interpretation

What lambda does to each direction

Filter factors

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Tikhonov in SVD Coordinates
Let

A v_i = \sigma_i u_i.

Then

x_\lambda
=
\sum_i
\frac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_i^2+\lambda}
\langle y,u_i\rangle v_i.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Compare with Direct Inversion
Direct inverse factor:

\frac{1}{\sigma_i}.

Tikhonov factor:

\frac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_i^2+\lambda}.


When \sigma_i is small, Tikhonov avoids division by a tiny number.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text: Filter Factors

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Reading the Filter Factors


Small \lambda behaves more like the inverse.
Larger \lambda damps weak singular directions.
Strong damping reduces noise but also removes detail.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Activity 3: Direction-by-Direction

6 minutes


For \sigma=0.001, compare:

\frac{1}{\sigma}
\quad\text{and}\quad
\frac{\sigma}{\sigma^2+\lambda}

for \lambda=0.01.
What changed?

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Part 4: Choosing Lambda

No free lunch

The parameter is the lesson

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Parameter Effects


Small \lambda:
better data fit, more noise amplification.


Large \lambda:
more stability, more bias and blur.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text: Error and L-Curve Views

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Important Reality
The true image is usually unknown.
So the RMSE curve is a teaching diagnostic, not a practical parameter-choice method.

In practice, parameter choice may use validation data, discrepancy principles, cross-validation, or L-curve heuristics.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text: Real Image Example

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Discussion

4 minutes


Which reconstruction would you choose visually?
Would your answer change if the image were for medical diagnosis?

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Part 5: Bias-Variance Tradeoff

What regularization buys and costs

Stability has a price

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Bias and Variance
Imagine repeating the noisy measurement many times.


Bias:
systematic error from the average reconstruction.


Variance:
random variation across noise realizations.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text: Bias-Variance Picture

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Reading the Tradeoff


Increasing \lambda usually reduces variance.
Increasing \lambda usually increases bias.
The best mean squared error is often between the extremes.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
Activity 4: Explain the U-Shape

5 minutes


Why can the error be large for both very small and very large \lambda?
Use the words “noise”, “bias”, and “stability”.

title: Tikhonov Regularization
text:
What Students Should Remember


Tikhonov solves \min_x \|Ax-y\|_2^2+\lambda\|x\|_2^2.
The normal equations are (A^TA+\lambda I)x=A^Ty.
In SVD coordinates, Tikhonov damps weak singular directions.
\lambda controls a bias-stability tradeoff.
Regularization is not magic; it encodes a modeling choice.

text:
Opening Question

Edges are not noise

How can a regularizer remove noise without treating every edge as a mistake?

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
Today


Define Total Variation (TV).
Compare TV with quadratic smoothness.
Explain why TV preserves edges better.
Observe the staircasing effect.
Use TV denoising on signals and images.

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
75-Minute Plan



Time
Work




0-8 min
recap variational energy models

edge preservation in 1D

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
Bridge from Week 7
Week 7 used the quadratic smoothing energy:

E(u)=\frac12\|u-y\|_2^2+\frac{\lambda}{2}\|\nabla u\|_2^2.


What happens to true edges when we penalize large gradients quadratically?

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
The Problem with Quadratic Smoothing
Edges are large gradients.
Noise also creates large local changes.

Quadratic smoothness reduces both noise and edges.

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
Part 1: Total Variation

A different smoothness prior

Penalize gradient magnitude

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
Continuous TV
For a grayscale image u:\Omega\to\mathbb{R}:

\operatorname{TV}(u)
=
\int_\Omega |\nabla u(x)|\,dx.


TV measures the total amount of change in the image.

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
Discrete Isotropic TV
For a pixel grid:

\operatorname{TV}(u)
=
\sum_i
\sqrt{(D_xu_i)^2+(D_yu_i)^2}.


D_x and D_y are finite differences in horizontal and vertical directions.

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
TV Denoising Model
The classical Rudin-Osher-Fatemi model is:

u_\lambda
=
\operatorname*{argmin}_u
\frac12\|u-y\|_2^2
+
\lambda\,\operatorname{TV}(u).


Data fidelity keeps the image close to data; TV regularization controls total edge strength.

title: Total Variation Regularization
text: l2 Versus TV Penalties

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
Why TV Preserves Edges
Quadratic penalty:

\frac12 g^2

TV penalty:

|g|.


Large gradients are much more expensive under \ell_2 than under TV.

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
Activity 1: Penalty Comparison

5 minutes


Compare the cost of a gradient g=4:

quadratic cost \frac12 g^2;
TV cost |g|.

Which penalty discourages a sharp edge more strongly?

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
Nonsmoothness
The function |g| is not differentiable at g=0.

Why might this make optimization more delicate than quadratic smoothing?


Week 9 will discuss subgradients and proximal ideas.

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
Part 2: 1D Edge Preservation

Start with a signal

Jumps are allowed

title: Total Variation Regularization
text: Step Signal Comparison

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
Reading the Step Example


The noisy signal has many small oscillations.
l2 smoothing rounds the jumps.
TV denoising keeps flatter regions and sharper jumps.
TV is well matched to piecewise-constant structure.

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
Why Piecewise Constant?
TV counts the amount of change, not how spread out it is.

A sharp jump can be cheaper than a long gradual transition with many small changes.

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
Activity 2: Predict the Prior

5 minutes


Which image class does TV prefer?

smooth ramps everywhere;
piecewise flat regions separated by edges;
high-frequency texture everywhere.

Give a one-sentence reason.

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
Part 3: Image Denoising

Real image comparison

Noise versus edge preservation

title: Total Variation Regularization
text: l2 Smoothing Versus TV

title: Total Variation Regularization
text: Gradient Maps

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
Reading Gradient Maps


Noise creates many small gradients.
l2 smoothing reduces gradients broadly.
TV suppresses weak fluctuations while retaining stronger edge sets.

title: Total Variation Regularization
text: Parameter Effects

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
Regularization Weight
In

\frac12\|u-y\|_2^2+\lambda\operatorname{TV}(u),

larger \lambda means:


stronger denoising;
fewer small variations;
greater risk of losing detail.

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
Activity 3: Choose Lambda

5 minutes


For the parameter-effect figure, choose a TV weight.
Would you choose the same value for:

a diagnostic medical image;
a photograph for visual display?

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
Part 4: Staircasing

TV has a signature artifact

Piecewise-flat plateaus

title: Total Variation Regularization
text: Staircasing Effect

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
Why Staircasing Happens
TV favors sparse gradients.
That means many locations with zero gradient and a smaller number of jumps.

TV preserves edges, but it can turn smooth variation into plateaus.

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
Strengths and Weaknesses








Property
l2 smoothing
TV




Noise removal
yes
yes


Edge preservation
weak
strong


Optimization
smooth linear problem
nonsmooth convex problem


Typical artifact
blur
staircasing


Best for
smooth images
piecewise-smooth or piecewise-constant images

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
Part 5: Modeling View

What prior did we choose?

Regularization is a modeling statement

title: Total Variation Regularization
text:
TV as a Prior
TV regularization says:

Among images that fit the data, prefer images with small total variation.

This is a good prior when meaningful structures are separated by edges.

TV is a variational prior favoring small total change.
Compared with l2 smoothing, TV preserves stronger edges.

The choice of regularizer is a modeling decision.

text:
Opening Question

Too few measurements

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
Part 1: Sparsity

Most coefficients are zero

But only after choosing a representation

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
Sparse Vector
A vector x\in\mathbb{R}^n is k-sparse if it has at most k nonzero entries.

\|x\|_0
=
\#\{i:x_i\neq 0\}.


\|\cdot\|_0 counts nonzeros; it is not a true norm.

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text: Sparse Versus Dense

title: Sparse Reconstruction
Sparse Reconstruction
text:
Approximate Sparsity
Natural images often have many small transform coefficients and a few large ones.

Sparse reconstruction does not require exact zeros everywhere; it often relies on coefficients that decay quickly.

title: Sparse Reconstruction
Sparse Reconstruction
text:
Part 2: l1 Versus l2

Two priors, different geometry

Spread energy or select coefficients

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
l2 Regularization
Tikhonov-style regularization uses

\frac12\|Ax-y\|_2^2+\frac{\lambda}{2}\|x\|_2^2.


l2 regularization prefers small energy spread across coefficients.

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
l1 Regularization
Sparse reconstruction often uses

\frac12\|Ax-y\|_2^2+\lambda\|x\|_1.

where

\|x\|_1=\sum_i |x_i|.


l1 regularization encourages exact zeros.

title: Sparse Reconstruction
section: Why l0 Is Not the Default
text:
Why l0 Is Not the Default
The ideal sparse model might be:

\min_x \|x\|_0
\quad\text{subject to}\quad
Ax=y.

But this is combinatorial.

l1 is the convex relaxation that keeps much of the sparse behavior.

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text: Geometry of l1 and l2

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
Reading the Geometry
The l2 ball is round.
The l1 ball has corners on coordinate axes.

When a constraint first touches an l1 ball, it often touches a corner, which means some coordinates are exactly zero.

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
Activity 2: Before Seeing the Answer

4 minutes


Suppose many vectors fit the same measurements.
Would l2 or l1 be more likely to choose a vector with only a few nonzero coefficients? Why?

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
Part 3: Underdetermined Recovery

More unknowns than equations

The prior chooses among many solutions

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
The Linear Model
We observe

y=Ax,

where A has fewer rows than columns.

If m<n, why should we expect many possible solutions?

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
Minimum l2-Norm Solution
One classical choice is:

\min_x \|x\|_2
\quad\text{subject to}\quad
Ax=y.

This gives the solution with smallest Euclidean norm.

It fits the data, but it may be dense.

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
l1-Regularized Solution
Instead solve:

\min_x
\frac12\|Ax-y\|_2^2+\lambda\|x\|_1.

This balances:


fitting the measurements;
keeping the coefficient vector sparse.

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text: Same Measurements, Different Prior

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
What Happened?
Both methods see the same A and y.
The difference is the prior.

l2 asks for a small vector; l1 asks for a sparse explanation.

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
Important Caveat
l1 does not magically recover every sparse signal.
It needs enough measurements and a measurement operator that mixes information well.

Sparse recovery depends on the pair: sparsity model plus measurement design.

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
Part 4: Compressed Sensing Intuition

Sampling below the apparent dimension

When structure reduces degrees of freedom

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
Degrees of Freedom
If x\in\mathbb{R}^{1000} is exactly 5-sparse, it is not truly a 1000-degree-of-freedom object.
We need to learn:


which 5 locations are active;
the 5 active values.

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
Measurement Count
Sparse recovery can work with far fewer than n measurements.
But not usually as few as k.

We need enough measurements to identify both support and amplitudes robustly.

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text: Measurement Sweep

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
Reading the Sweep


With very few measurements, both methods struggle.
After a threshold, l1 can recover the sparse vector well.
The l2 solution remains dense because its prior is different.

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
Activity 3: Failure Modes

5 minutes


Give one reason sparse recovery could fail even when the true signal is sparse.

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
Part 5: Sparse Images

Images are sparse after transformation

DCT example before wavelets

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
DCT Compression
The Discrete Cosine Transform represents an image patch as cosine patterns.
Many image patches can be approximated using only a small fraction of DCT coefficients.

Wavelets in Week 11 will give a more imaging-focused multiscale representation.

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text: Few Coefficients, Good Approximation

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
Sparse Reconstruction from Pixels
Suppose we observe only a random subset of pixels.
We can reconstruct by solving:

\min_c
\frac12\|M D^{-1}c-y\|_2^2+\lambda\|c\|_1.


D^{-1}c maps DCT coefficients back to an image; M keeps observed pixels.

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text: Random Pixels Plus Sparse Prior

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
What This Does Not Say
This is not a universal promise.


The representation must be appropriate.
The measurements must contain useful information.
The regularization weight matters.
Real systems include noise, calibration, and model mismatch.

title: Sparse Reconstruction
text:
What Students Should Remember


Sparsity is representation-dependent.
l1 is a convex surrogate for sparse selection.
l1 and l2 encode different priors.
Compressed sensing works when measurements and sparse model are compatible.
Sparse priors can reconstruct images from incomplete data, but only under assumptions.

Write the assumptions

Forward model plus prior

title: Model-Based vs Data-Driven Imaging
text:
Model-Based Template
A classical reconstruction often solves

\hat{x}
=
\operatorname*{argmin}_x
D(Ax,y)+\lambda R(x).


D is data fidelity; R is the regularizer or prior.

Part 2: Data-Driven Imaging

Learn the assumptions

Training data plus loss

title: Model-Based vs Data-Driven Imaging
text:
Data-Driven Template
Instead of writing the reconstruction rule by hand, learn a function:

\hat{x}=f_\theta(y).

The parameters \theta are chosen from training data.

title: Model-Based vs Data-Driven Imaging
text:
Supervised Training
With training pairs (y_i,x_i):

\min_\theta
\sum_i
\ell(f_\theta(y_i),x_i).


The loss tells the network what counts as a good reconstruction on the training set.

title: Model-Based vs Data-Driven Imaging
Model-Based vs Data-Driven Imaging
text:
Learned Prior View
A learned method can encode image structure that is hard to write as a simple formula.

The prior is no longer a short expression like TV; it is represented by parameters learned from examples.

Why Nonlinearity Matters
A composition of only linear layers is still linear.
Nonlinear activations allow the map to adapt to image content.

Why might a nonlinear reconstruction operator be useful for images?

text:
Neural Networks and Forward Models
Some methods ignore the explicit forward model:

\hat{x}=f_\theta(y).

Others include it:

x^{k+1}
=
\text{learned step using } A,\ A^\top,\ y.

The learned prior was trained on one image family.

Generalization is not a side issue; it is central to learned imaging.

text: Reliability Is Tested

title: Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
text:
Part 1: Stability

Small input, small output?

The first trust question

title: Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
text:
Stability
An imaging method is stable if small changes in the data tend to produce small changes in the reconstruction.

Stability is about sensitivity to perturbations: noise, calibration error, numerical error, or small modeling changes.

title: Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
text: Parameter Sensitivity

title: Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
text:
Activity 1: Parameter Choice

5 minutes


Suppose two reconstructions have similar visual quality.
One has lower data residual. The other is smoother and more stable under noise.
Which would you choose, and what extra test would you ask for?

title: Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
text:
Part 2: Perturbation Amplification

Invisible changes can matter

Worst-case directions

title: Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
text:
A Small Perturbation Test
Take an observation y and add a tiny perturbation \delta y.
Compare:

\frac{\|\delta y\|}{\|y\|}
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\frac{\|\hat{x}(y+\delta y)-\hat{x}(y)\|}{\|\hat{x}(y)\|}.


The output-change/input-change ratio is a practical sensitivity diagnostic.

title: Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
text: Amplification Example

title: Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
text:
Why This Happens
In singular-value language, unstable directions have small singular values.
Direct inversion multiplies by:

\frac{1}{\sigma_i}.

Regularization limits that multiplication.

title: Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
section: Stability Is Not Only About Noise

title: Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
text:
Part 3: Robustness

Does it still work off the happy path?

Beyond one clean experiment

title: Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
text:
Robustness
A method is robust if it works acceptably across a meaningful range of conditions.

Robustness is not a single number. It is a testing plan over expected and stressful cases.

title: Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
text:
A Minimal Robustness Table



Test
Question




noise sweep
Does performance degrade smoothly?


parameter sweep
Is there a wide safe range?


model mismatch
What if A is wrong?


image family shift
Does the prior overfit one domain?


small feature test
Are weak structures preserved?

title: Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
text: Domain Robustness

title: Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
text:
Reading the Domain Test
The learned patch prior was trained on camera-like patches.
It helps on a camera crop.
It is less competitive on a different image family.

Learning from data does not remove assumptions. It moves assumptions into the training set.

title: Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
text:
Part 4: Hallucination and Small Features

Plausible is not the same as true

Image quality can deceive

title: Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
text:
Hallucination
In reconstruction, hallucination means producing structure that looks plausible but is not supported by the measurements.

What is worse: inventing a false feature, or removing a real weak feature?

title: Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
text: Small Feature Risk

title: Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
text:
Reading the Small Feature Example
The feature is visible in the clean image.
With blur and noise, it is hard to recover.
Strong regularization and smoothing make the image look calmer, but reduce feature contrast.

The right reconstruction depends on the task, not only on visual smoothness.

title: Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
text:
Data Consistency Is Necessary
An output should be compatible with the measurement model:

Ax \approx y.

But data consistency alone is not enough when the inverse problem is ill-posed.

Many images may explain the same measurement within noise tolerance.

title: Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
text:
Part 5: Ethics and Responsibility

Mathematics has consequences

Especially in scientific and medical imaging

title: Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
text:
Reliability in Medical Imaging
For medical or high-stakes scientific use, a reconstruction can influence decisions.
That means we should ask:


What evidence supports the output?
What could be hidden by the algorithm?
Who is affected by a false positive or false negative?
Can a user see uncertainty and limitations?

title: Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
text: Questions Before Deployment

title: Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
text:
Ethical Failure Modes


A model works worse for some populations or acquisition sites.
A reconstruction removes rare but important signs.
A plausible output is treated as direct measurement.
Users are not told when the method is outside its tested domain.
Code and parameters are not reproducible.

title: Stability, Robustness, and Ethics
text:
What Students Should Remember


Ill-posed inverse problems require stability checks.
A good-looking image can still be unreliable.
Learned priors inherit assumptions from training data.
Robustness must be tested across realistic and stressful cases.
Ethical reconstruction means documenting assumptions, failures, and limits.
